papers.title: Foundation-Model Surrogates Enable Data-Efficient Active Learning for Materials Discovery
papers.abstract: Active learning (AL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for accelerating materials discovery by iteratively steering experiments toward promising candidates, reducing the number of costly synthesis-and-characterization cycles needed to identify optimal materials. However, current AL relies predominantly on Gaussian Process (GP) and Random Forest (RF) surrogates, which suffer from complementary limitations: GP underfits complex composition-property landscapes due to rigid kernel assumptions, while RF produces unreliable heuristic uncertainty estimates in small-data regimes. This small-data challenge is pervasive in materials science, making reliable surrogate modeling extremely difficult with models trained from scratch on each new dataset. Here we propose In-Context Active Learning (ICAL), which addresses this bottleneck by replacing conventional surrogates with TabPFN, a transformer-based foundation model (FM) pre-trained on millions of synthetic regression tasks to meta-learn a universal prior over tabular data, upon which TabPFN performs principled Bayesian inference in a single forward pass without dataset-specific retraining, delivering strong small-data regression performance and well-calibrated predictive uncertainty (required for effective AL). We benchmark ICAL against GP and RF across 10 materials datasets and TabPFN wins on 8 out of 10 datasets, achieving a mean saving of 52% in extra evaluations relative to GP and 29.77% relative to RF. Cross-validation analysis confirms that TabPFN's advantage stems from superior uncertainty calibration, achieving the lowest Negative Log-Likelihood and Area Under the Sparsification Error curve among all surrogates. These results demonstrate that pre-trained FMs can serve as effective surrogates for active learning, enabling data-efficient discovery across diverse materials systems and small-data experimental sciences.

papers.title: Uncertainty Disentanglement with Non-stationary Heteroscedastic Gaussian Processes for Active Learning
papers.abstract: Gaussian processes are Bayesian non-parametric models used in many areas. In this work, we propose a Non-stationary Heteroscedastic Gaussian process model which can be learned with gradient-based techniques. We demonstrate the interpretability of the proposed model by separating the overall uncertainty into aleatoric (irreducible) and epistemic (model) uncertainty. We illustrate the usability of derived epistemic uncertainty on active learning problems. We demonstrate the efficacy of our model with various ablations on multiple datasets.

However, the paper focuses on the methodological advancements of the GP model itself (e.g., using Gibbs kernel, inducing point approximations, and gradient-based learning for hyperparameters) and demonstrates it on general regression and active learning tasks, rather than specifically applying it to alloy phase diagrams or DFT computations as in the user's idea. | Domain: The paper focuses on the methodological advancements of Gaussian processes for active learning, specifically disentangling uncertainty, and demonstrates its efficacy on general regression tasks and sensor placement. papers.title: Actively Learning Gaussian Process Dynamics
papers.abstract: Despite the availability of ever more data enabled through modern sensor and computer technology, it still remains an open problem to learn dynamical systems in a sample-efficient way. We propose active learning strategies that leverage information-theoretical properties arising naturally during Gaussian process regression, while respecting constraints on the sampling process imposed by the system dynamics. Sample points are selected in regions with high uncertainty, leading to exploratory behavior and data-efficient training of the model. All results are finally verified in an extensive numerical benchmark.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of sample-efficient learning of dynamical systems using active learning and Gaussian processes, selecting sample points in regions of high uncertainty. papers.title: Accurate and Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Prediction of HEA Properties Using Prior-Guided Deep Gaussian Processes
papers.abstract: Surrogate modeling techniques have become indispensable in accelerating the discovery and optimization of high-entropy alloys(HEAs), especially when integrating computational predictions with sparse experimental observations. This study systematically evaluates the fitting performance of four prominent surrogate models conventional Gaussian Processes(cGP), Deep Gaussian Processes(DGP), encoder-decoder neural networks for multi-output regression and XGBoost applied to a hybrid dataset of experimental and computational properties in the AlCoCrCuFeMnNiV HEA system. We specifically assess their capabilities in predicting correlated material properties, including yield strength, hardness, modulus, ultimate tensile strength, elongation, and average hardness under dynamic and quasi-static conditions, alongside auxiliary computational properties. The comparison highlights the strengths of hierarchical and deep modeling approaches in handling heteroscedastic, heterotopic, and incomplete data commonly encountered in materials informatics. Our findings illustrate that DGP infused with machine learning-based prior outperform other surrogates by effectively capturing inter-property correlations and input-dependent uncertainty. This enhanced predictive accuracy positions advanced surrogate models as powerful tools for robust and data-efficient materials design.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of predicting HEA properties using surrogate models, specifically evaluating Deep Gaussian Processes for multi-task prediction and uncertainty quantification. The user's idea specifically mentions 'active learning with Gaussian process uncertainty to select which alloy compositions to compute via DFT next,' while the paper focuses on 'Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs)' for 'multi-task prediction of HEA properties' and discusses their 'uncertainty-awareness' and use within a 'BO loop' (Bayesian optimization, a form of active learning). However, the paper's primary focus is on evaluating different surrogate models for property prediction and incorporating prior knowledge, rather than the specific active learning strategy for phase diagram mapping via DFT as outlined by the user. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper are within the same discipline of materials science, specifically materials informatics and alloy design. The paper, while using Deep Gaussian Processes with uncertainty quantification for materials, applies it to predict properties of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) from a hybrid dataset and does not mention active learning for DFT calculations or mapping phase diagrams. The general direction of using GPs for materials property prediction is similar, but the specific application and methodology for contribution are different.

papers.title: Optimal experimental design via Bayesian optimization: active causal structure learning for Gaussian process networks
papers.abstract: We study the problem of causal discovery through targeted interventions. Starting from few observational measurements, we follow a Bayesian active learning approach to perform those experiments which, in expectation with respect to the current model, are maximally informative about the underlying causal structure. Unlike previous work, we consider the setting of continuous random variables with non-linear functional relationships, modelled with Gaussian process priors. To address the arising problem of choosing from an uncountable set of possible interventions, we propose to use Bayesian optimisation to efficiently maximise a Monte Carlo estimate of the expected information gain.

The core methodology of using GPs for active learning is similar, but the specific problem formulation and application domain differ significantly. | Domain: The user's idea is in materials science (alloy phase diagrams, DFT calculations), while the paper is in the general domain of causal discovery and experimental design, with examples like a robot exploring its surroundings or a scientist in a lab. Both involve active learning and Gaussian processes for scientific discovery, placing them in the same discipline (machine learning/statistics for scientific applications) but different sub-areas and application targets. | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is 'the first work to address the Bayesian experimental design problem for continuous variables, allowing for flexible non-linear relationships through the use of GP networks' and 'actively choosing interventions using an information theoretic approach' (Related work). existing_examples.name: Active Learning for Materials Discovery
existing_examples.description: Numerous research groups and publications have explored active learning, often with Gaussian processes, to accelerate materials discovery and optimization, including predicting phase stability and properties. This directly applies to reducing DFT calculations for phase diagrams.

existing_examples.name: Bayesian Optimization in Materials Science
existing_examples.description: Bayesian optimization, which commonly uses Gaussian processes to model objective functions and acquisition functions (like UCB or Expected Improvement, related to uncertainty), is a well-established technique in materials science for optimizing synthesis, properties, and computational exploration.

existing_examples.name: CALPHAD (CALculation of PHAse Diagrams) with Machine Learning
existing_examples.description: While CALPHAD itself is an older method, modern approaches often integrate machine learning, including active learning strategies, to more efficiently generate or refine thermodynamic databases and phase diagrams, sometimes leveraging DFT data.

matched_sections.paper_title: Actively Learning Gaussian Process Dynamics
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: We propose active learning strategies that leverage information-theoretical properties arising naturally during Gaussian process regression, while respecting constraints on the sampling process imposed by the system dynamics. Sample points are selected in regions with high uncertainty, leading to exploratory behavior and data-efficient training of the model.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'active learning with Gaussian process uncertainty' to select compositions, which directly aligns with the paper's core methodology of using active learning and Gaussian process regression, selecting sample points in regions of high uncertainty for data-efficient training.

matched_sections.paper_title: Actively Learning Gaussian Process Dynamics
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: We consider Gaussian process (GP) regression, which has been proven to be an efficient framework in many related applications, including model learning (Nguyen-Tuong and Peters, 2011) or reinforcement learning (Deisenroth and Rasmussen, 2011; Doerr et al., 2017). These probabilistic models have many advantageous properties for learning dynamical systems (Jain et al., 2018; Eleftheriadis et al., 2017; Doerr et al., 2018), such as taking uncertainty into account, coping with small datasets and incorporating prior knowledge. Active learning, i.e., sequentially choosing where to sample in order to build an informative dataset, has been investigated in many domains (see (Aggarwal, 2014) for an overview).
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes 'Using active learning with Gaussian process uncertainty'. The paper extensively discusses Gaussian process regression and active learning as a method for sequentially choosing where to sample, highlighting its ability to take uncertainty into account.

matched_sections.paper_title: Actively Learning Gaussian Process Dynamics
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Indeed, in the case of physical systems, sampling data often requires practically involved and time-consuming experiments. Further, sampling at informative locations of the state space is challenging, since the system is constrained by the underlying dynamics. Hence, it is essential to excite the system in such a way that the generated data enables sample-efficient learning.
matched_sections.reason: The user's problem of 'reducing the number of calculations needed' for DFT computations aligns with the paper's general problem of 'sample-efficient learning' in time-consuming experiments, though the specific application domain differs.

Sample points are selected in regions with high uncertainty, leading to exploratory behavior and data-efficient training of the model.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea aims to 'reduce the number of calculations needed' by selecting compositions based on uncertainty. This aligns with the paper's contribution of achieving 'data-efficient training of the model' by selecting sample points in regions of high uncertainty.

matched_sections.paper_title: Actively Learning Gaussian Process Dynamics
matched_sections.heading: Problem statement
matched_sections.text_snippet: We address the following question: how should one excite (1) to generate samples ( Z, Y ) for learning f in a sample-efficient way? We derive control inputs that optimize information criteria such as differential entropy, while taking the autoregressive structure of (1) explicitly into account. Ultimately, this reduces the prediction error given a fixed number of samples, by choosing informative control inputs that lead to exploratory system behavior.
matched_sections.reason: The user's goal of 'reducing the number of calculations needed' is a form of sample-efficient learning, which is a direct contribution of the paper: 'generate samples... in a sample-efficient way' and 'reduces the prediction error given a fixed number of samples'.

layer1_results.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of predicting HEA properties using surrogate models, specifically evaluating Deep Gaussian Processes for multi-task prediction and uncertainty quantification. While both the user's idea and the paper use Gaussian processes and aim to reduce computational effort in materials science, the user's idea specifically focuses on active learning to select alloy compositions for DFT calculations to map binary alloy phase diagrams, which is a different specific problem than the paper's focus on property prediction in a complex HEA system. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper utilize Gaussian Processes (GPs) and uncertainty quantification in materials science. The user's idea specifically mentions 'active learning with Gaussian process uncertainty to select which alloy compositions to compute via DFT next,' while the paper focuses on 'Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs)' for 'multi-task prediction of HEA properties' and discusses their 'uncertainty-awareness' and use within a 'BO loop' (Bayesian optimization, a form of active learning). However, the paper's primary focus is on evaluating different surrogate models for property prediction and incorporating prior knowledge, rather than the specific active learning strategy for phase diagram mapping via DFT as outlined by the user. | Domain: Both the user's idea and the paper are within the same discipline of materials science, specifically materials informatics and alloy design. However, the user's idea focuses on mapping binary alloy phase diagrams using DFT calculations, while the paper focuses on predicting properties of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) using a hybrid dataset of experimental and computational properties. | Contribution: The user's idea focuses on using active learning with Gaussian process uncertainty to select alloy compositions for DFT calculations to map binary alloy phase diagrams. The paper, while using Deep Gaussian Processes with uncertainty quantification for materials, applies it to predict properties of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) from a hybrid dataset and does not mention active learning for DFT calculations or mapping phase diagrams. The general direction of using GPs for materials property prediction is similar, but the specific application and methodology for contribution are different.

matched_sections.paper_title: Accurate and Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Prediction of HEA Properties Using Prior-Guided Deep Gaussian Processes
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Surrogate modeling techniques have become indispensable in accelerating the discovery and optimization of high-entropy alloys(HEAs), especially when integrating computational predictions with sparse experimental observations.

matched_sections.paper_title: Accurate and Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Prediction of HEA Properties Using Prior-Guided Deep Gaussian Processes
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Surrogate models serve as computationally efficient approximations of complex structure-property relationships and are widely used to accelerate materials discovery. Gaussian Processes (GPs) provide calibrated uncertainty estimates, making them effective for sparse, high-fidelity datasets [7], while multi-task extensions and co-kriging allow for correlated output modeling [8].

matched_sections.paper_title: Accurate and Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Prediction of HEA Properties Using Prior-Guided Deep Gaussian Processes
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Gaussian Processes (GPs) provide calibrated uncertainty estimates, making them effective for sparse, high-fidelity datasets [7], while multi-task extensions and co-kriging allow for correlated output modeling [8]. Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) extend this capability to hierarchical, nonlinear functions [9], offering an advantage in capturing complex material behavior.

matched_sections.paper_title: Accurate and Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Prediction of HEA Properties Using Prior-Guided Deep Gaussian Processes
matched_sections.heading: Deep Gaussian Processes (DGP)
matched_sections.text_snippet: Building directly on the fundamental concepts introduced above, Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) extend conventional GPs by incorporating a hierarchical structure. linked_sections.paper_title: Foundation-Model Surrogates Enable Data-Efficient Active Learning for Materials Discovery
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Active learning (AL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for accelerating materials discovery by iteratively steering experiments toward promising candidates, reducing the number of costly synthesis-and-characterization cycles needed to identify optimal materials.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper aim to reduce the number of calculations/evaluations needed for materials discovery using active learning.

linked_sections.paper_title: Foundation-Model Surrogates Enable Data-Efficient Active Learning for Materials Discovery
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: However, current AL relies predominantly on Gaussian Process (GP) and Random Forest (RF) surrogates, which suffer from complementary limitations: GP underfits complex composition-property landscapes due to rigid kernel assumptions, while RF produces unreliable heuristic uncertainty estimates in small-data regimes.
linked_sections.reason: The user explicitly mentions 'Gaussian process uncertainty' as part of their method, which is a key method discussed and benchmarked in the paper.
